steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures on each side of the blank
steps.content: On the left: “What increases is the rate at which the water turns to [blank]” is a complete independent clause (about the uncovered pot scenario). On the right: “an airtight seal traps the vapor in the pot” is another complete independent clause (about the pressure cooker scenario).

steps.title: Apply the sentence boundary rule
steps.content:
Two independent clauses must be separated by a period. Choice A correctly places “vapor.” to end the first sentence about the uncovered pot, then starts a new sentence: “With a pressure cooker pot, though, an airtight seal traps...,” where “though” is a parenthetical word (set off by commas) showing the contrast.

Choices B, C, and D attempt to join the clauses improperly with ungrammatical phrases like “vapor with,” “vapor, with,” or “vapor and with.” That matches choice A — A is correct.
